topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notice sat on the chipped Formica table, a stark white rectangle against the muted tones of my cramped dorm room. I traced the embossed lettering with a fingertip, the paper feeling strangely brittle under my touch. It was done. Northwood University was folding. I didn't move to pick up my backpack; the weight of it felt suddenly enormous, a tether to a place that was dissolving. I stared out the window at the quad, where the autumn leaves were already turning brittle and falling, mirroring the way the energy had leached out of everything here. I just sat there, letting the silence of the impending shutdown settle over me, a heavy, unmoving blanket.

[story 2]

"So, we're really packing up everything?" I asked, my voice sounding thin, almost swallowed by the echo in the empty lecture hall. Professor Albright just sighed, a long, weary sound that seemed to carry the weight of years of academic disappointment. I watched the dust motes dance in the single shaft of weak afternoon light cutting across the floorboards. There was no surge of panic, just a slow, deliberate letting go. I gathered my worn textbooks, placing them carefully into a box, the movement economical and devoid of enthusiasm. It wasn't a shock; it was just the final acknowledgment of an ending, a quiet settling into a predetermined state of acceptance.

[story 3]

I walked through the familiar hallways one last time, the fluorescent lights buzzing with a monotonous, irritating frequency. Every corner held a ghost of a memory—a late-night study session, a shared laugh over terrible cafeteria food. Now, those memories didn't spark warmth; they just registered as faded photographs. When the final announcement came, I didn't flinch. I stopped by the main administrative office, leaned against the cool glass of the doorframe, and simply waited for the inevitable paperwork. My shoulders dropped slightly, a subtle sinking that spoke volumes. I pulled my phone out, not to call anyone, but just to watch the screen glow, an inert object in my hand.
